The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\n   [verified] / removes it.\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.started\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"\",\"exit_code\":null,\"status\":\"in_progress\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_7\",\"type\":\"command_execution\",\"command\":\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\"\",\"aggregated_output\":\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\n\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\n\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. The Concept\\n\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\n\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\n\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\n\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\n\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\n\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\" inside\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\nfor the architecture.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\n\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\n\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\n\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\n\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\n\\n### Where the \\\"genuinely interesting\\\" lives\\n\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\n   The Director's job description is \\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\" not \\\"be hard\\\" — surprise,\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\"Show me what the AI\\n   invented for me and why\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\"dungeon diary\\\"\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\n\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\n\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\n\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\n\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\n\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\nlanguage (the \\\"floor manifest\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\n\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\n\\n### The freedom spectrum\\n\\n\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\nlives:\\n\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\n|---|---|\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\n\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\n\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\n   maximum depth per run.\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\n\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\n\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\n\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. It explains what we are building, why it is\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 1. The Concept\\n\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\n\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\n\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\n\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\n\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\n\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\" inside\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\nfor the architecture.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\n\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\n\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\n\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\n\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\n\\n### Where the \\\"genuinely interesting\\\" lives\\n\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\n   The Director's job description is \\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\" not \\\"be hard\\\" — surprise,\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\"Show me what the AI\\n   invented for me and why\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\"dungeon diary\\\"\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\n\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\n\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\n\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\n\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\n\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\nlanguage (the \\\"floor manifest\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\n\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\n\\n### The freedom spectrum\\n\\n\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\nlives:\\n\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\n|---|---|\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\n\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\n\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\n   maximum depth per run.\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\n\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\n\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\n\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\n\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\n\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\n\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\n\",\"exit_code\":0,\"status\":\"completed\"}}\n{\"type\":\"item.completed\",\"item\":{\"id\":\"item_8\",\"type\":\"agent_message\",\"text\":\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. The Phase 01 environment spike walks every [inherited] fact and flips it to\\\\n   [verified] / removes it.\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.started\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_7\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":null,\\\"status\\\":\\\"in_progress\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_7\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"command_execution\\\",\\\"command\\\":\\\"/bin/zsh -lc \\\\\\\"sed -n '1,180p' NORTH_STAR.md\\\\\\\"\\\",\\\"aggregated_output\\\":\\\"# Generative Gaming — North Star\\\\n\\\\n> **Can an AI agent author a living game world at runtime — items, enemies, quests, NPCs,\\\\n> story — with enough freedom to genuinely surprise the player, while a deterministic\\\\n> engine and an eval harness keep the game playable, fair, and finite?**\\\\n\\\\nThis document is the project's fixed point. It explains what we are building, why it is\\\\ninteresting, and what must never break. It deliberately stays at low technical resolution:\\\\nimplementation contracts live in `AGENTS.md` and `phase-plans/` (to be authored next).\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 1. The Concept\\\\n\\\\nWe are building a **Mystery Dungeon-style (不思議なダンジョン) roguelike where the dungeon\\\\nis authored, floor by floor, by an AI Director while you play it.**\\\\n\\\\nIn a classic Mystery Dungeon game, procedural generation shuffles a fixed deck: the same\\\\nenemies, items, and floor layouts in new arrangements. Here, the deck itself is generative.\\\\nThe AI Director invents new items, enemies, quests, NPCs, floor themes, and story beats at\\\\nruntime — but it can only express them through a strict, schema-validated content language\\\\nthat a deterministic engine executes. The AI proposes; the engine disposes.\\\\n\\\\nThe one-line pitch: **a dungeon that reads you back.**\\\\n\\\\nThe Director watches *how* you actually play — your trace, not your words. Hoard\\\\nconsumables and it sends enemies that punish hoarding and an NPC who mocks your full\\\\npockets. Flee every fight and the dungeon grows narrow and pursuing. Talk to every NPC and\\\\nthe story deepens around the characters you favored. Die, and your next run descends into a\\\\ndungeon that remembers the last one. No two players experience the same dungeon, because\\\\nthe dungeon is, in a real sense, *about them*.\\\\n\\\\n### Working fiction (revisable)\\\\n\\\\nWorking title: **Everdeep**. The dungeon is a vast, formless entity that shapes itself\\\\naround each delver who enters — part labyrinth, part storyteller, part adversary. The\\\\ndeeper you go, the more it knows you, and the more personal its inventions become. The\\\\nfiction is intentionally thin scaffolding: it justifies \\\\\\\"the dungeon adapts to you\\\\\\\" inside\\\\nthe story itself, so the AI's authorship is a feature of the world rather than a mechanic\\\\nbolted onto it. Tone, naming, and story arc are decided later and are not load-bearing\\\\nfor the architecture.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 2. Why This Genre (Bottom-Up Reasoning)\\\\n\\\\nWe chose the genre from technical constraints first, exactly as instructed, and then made\\\\nsure the constraints themselves produce the fun.\\\\n\\\\nA turn-based, grid-based, tile/text-rendered roguelike is the genre where today's LLMs and\\\\nagent tooling are strongest and weakest in the right places:\\\\n\\\\n- **Turn-based** — no real-time latency pressure. The Director can think for seconds\\\\n  between floors without the player ever waiting, because generation happens in the\\\\n  background while the current floor is being played.\\\\n- **Grid + structured actions** — game state is small, serializable, and inspectable.\\\\n  Both humans and bot players consume the same structured action interface, which makes\\\\n  automated evaluation (and agent-driven QA) cheap and honest.\\\\n- **Seeded determinism in the engine** — the engine's randomness is reproducible. The only\\\\n  nondeterministic component is the Director, and its output is captured as an artifact, so\\\\n  every run can be replayed exactly.\\\\n- **Floor boundaries** — natural generation checkpoints. Each floor is a self-contained\\\\n  content unit that can be validated, simulated, and rejected *before* the player sets foot\\\\n  on it. This is what makes runtime AI freedom safe.\\\\n- **Tile/text-first rendering** — content is data, not assets. An AI-invented enemy needs a\\\\n  glyph, stats, and behavior parameters — not a sprite sheet. This is what makes runtime AI\\\\n  freedom *cheap*.\\\\n\\\\nThe genre's native expectations also align with generative content: roguelike players\\\\nalready expect unfamiliar items with unknown effects, identify-by-use, permadeath, and\\\\nruns that tell their own stories. AI-invented content feels like a deepening of the genre,\\\\nnot a gimmick on top of it.\\\\n\\\\n### Where the \\\\\\\"genuinely interesting\\\\\\\" lives\\\\n\\\\n1. **Gameplay**: adaptive content keeps the difficulty curve and tactical texture personal.\\\\n   The Director's job description is \\\\\\\"be a great dungeon master,\\\\\\\" not \\\\\\\"be hard\\\\\\\" — surprise,\\\\n   tempo, and fairness over raw challenge.\\\\n2. **Scenario**: emergent personal narrative. Quests, NPCs, and story beats are written in\\\\n   response to your actual deeds, and the dungeon's memory persists across runs. The story\\\\n   is not branching — it is *grown*.\\\\n3. **Spectacle**: every floor manifest is an inspectable artifact. \\\\\\\"Show me what the AI\\\\n   invented for me and why\\\\\\\" is itself part of the experience — a post-run \\\\\\\"dungeon diary\\\\\\\"\\\\n   that recaps what the Director made of you.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 3. The Two-Layer World Model\\\\n\\\\nThe architecture has exactly one big idea, inherited from our reference projects and\\\\nsharpened: **separate creative intent from world physics.**\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 1 — The Engine (deterministic, hand-written, trusted)\\\\n\\\\nA finite, turn-based, seeded roguelike engine. It owns movement, combat resolution, line of\\\\nsight, item effects resolution, status effects, hunger/resources, win/loss, and rendering.\\\\nIt is ordinary, well-tested software. It never calls an LLM. The game is fully playable\\\\noffline against a built-in fallback content pack — the AI is an enhancement, never a\\\\nruntime dependency for taking the next turn.\\\\n\\\\n### Layer 2 — The Director (generative, AI, untrusted)\\\\n\\\\nAn LLM agent that authors content *between* floors, expressed only in a bounded content\\\\nlanguage (the \\\\\\\"floor manifest\\\\\\\"): layout parameters, enemy definitions, item definitions,\\\\nNPC definitions with finite dialogue, quest definitions, event scripts, and narration. The\\\\nDirector reads the player's trace and the run's memory, and writes the next floor.\\\\n\\\\nEvery manifest passes through a gauntlet before the player sees it (Section 5). A manifest\\\\nthat fails any gate is repaired or replaced by fallback content — the player never sees a\\\\nbroken floor, only a slightly less personal one.\\\\n\\\\n### The freedom spectrum\\\\n\\\\n\\\\\\\"More freedom than Mystery Dungeon\\\\\\\" is the point, so we are explicit about where freedom\\\\nlives:\\\\n\\\\n| The Director MAY invent | The Director may NOT touch |\\\\n|---|---|\\\\n| New items: names, glyphs, effects composed from an effect vocabulary | The effect vocabulary itself (engine-owned verbs) |\\\\n| New enemies: stats, behavior parameters, abilities from a behavior vocabulary | Combat math, turn order, engine rules |\\\\n| New NPCs with finite dialogue and barter | Unbounded free-chat NPCs (post-MVP, behind the same gates) |\\\\n| Quests referencing real entities on real floors | Quests requiring entities that don't exist (integrity-checked) |\\\\n| Floor themes, layout parameters, events, narration | Map solvability (validated), player stats, inventory (engine-owned) |\\\\n| Story beats, callbacks, run-to-run memory hooks | Win/loss conditions, the existence of an ending |\\\\n\\\\nThe deliberate design bet: **composable vocabularies give the feeling of unbounded\\\\ninvention with the safety of bounded execution.** A vocabulary of a few dozen effect and\\\\nbehavior primitives, composed freely with AI-authored parameters, names, and fiction,\\\\nyields a combinatorial space no content team could exhaust — and every point in it is\\\\nsomething the engine already knows how to run.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 4. Core Invariants (Never Break These)\\\\n\\\\n1. The game is **finite** — explicit `WIN` / `LOSS` / `ABORTED` terminal states, fixed\\\\n   maximum depth per run.\\\\n2. The game is **turn-based** with **structured actions** — no real-time input, no\\\\n   free-text gameplay commands.\\\\n3. The engine is **deterministic and seeded** — identical seed + identical manifests +\\\\n   identical actions = identical run.\\\\n4. **AI output never mutates state directly.** It is parsed, validated, simulated, and\\\\n   applied by deterministic code, or it is discarded. The schema is the world's physics.\\\\n5. The game is **playable with zero API calls** — fallback content packs make the AI\\\\n   optional at runtime, mandatory only for the magic.\\\\n6. Every Director generation is **persisted as an inspectable artifact** — manifest in,\\\\n   gates passed, content out. Evidence over vibes.\\\\n7. **Playability is gated, not hoped for** — no floor reaches the player without passing\\\\n   the validation gauntlet.\\\\n8. The Director **cannot make the game unfinishable, unfair, or unfun by fiat** — bounds\\\\n   on stats, economy, and difficulty are enforced outside the model.\\\\n\\\\n---\\\\n\\\\n## 5. Harnessing the AI: The Gauntlet and the Evals\\\\n\\\\nThis is the project's center of gravity. Generative freedom is only worth having if the\\\\ngame stays playable, so the harness is a first-class product, not test infrastructure.\\\\n\\\\n### Runtime gauntlet (every floor manifest, every time)\\\\n\\\\n- **Gate 0 — Structure.** Schema validation. Malformed output is rejected outright.\\\\n- **Gate 1 — Legality.** Referential integrity (every quest target, NPC, and item the\\\\n  manifest mentions actually exists in it or in the run) and hard bounds (stats, economy\\\\n  values, counts, text lengths all inside engine-enforced ranges).\\\\n- **Gate 2 — Playability.** The floor is simulated before it is served: a path to the\\\\n  stairs exists, quest objectives are reachable, and a small ensemble of scripted bot\\\\n  players completes the floor within a survivability band. Floors that are unwinnable or\\\\n  trivially empty are rejected.\\\\n- **Gate 3 — Quality.** Coherence and novelty checks: tone fits the run's fiction, content\\\\n  is not a near-duplicate of recent floors, difficulty trend fits the run's curve. (Cheap\\\\n  heuristics first; LLM-judge where heuristics can't reach.)\\\\n- **Fallback.** A bounded repair loop, then graceful degradation to handcrafted content.\\\\n  Failure is invisible to the player and loud in the logs.\\\\n\\\\n### Offline eval suite (every change to prompts, models, or schemas)\\\\n\\\\nA standing benchmark, run in CI and before any Director change ships:\\\\n\\\\n- A bank of recorded player traces and personas (hoarder, pacifist, speedrunner,\\\\n\\\",\\\"exit_code\\\":0,\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\"}}\\n{\\\"type\\\":\\\"item.completed\\\",\\\"item\\\":{\\\"id\\\":\\\"item_8\\\",\\\"type\\\":\\\"agent_message\\\",\\\"text\\\":\\\"The queue already marks `01A-1` as in progress, and the current brief forbids edits outside the spike folder, so I’m not touching `PROGRESS.md`. runs/sessions/phase56-m2.jsonl:167:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_86","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc 'tail -n 120 runs/sessions/phase55-e2e-last.txt 2>/dev/null || true'","aggregated_output":"Implemented the Phase 55 e2e surface, but I cannot honestly mark the task done: `pnpm run e2e` cannot launch Chromium from this Codex sandbox, so the required 5 consecutive green runs were not achieved.

Changed:
- Added one Playwright spec: `e2e/happy-path.spec.ts`
- Added `playwright.config.ts`
- Added `package.json` `e2e` script and pinned `@playwright/test@1.58.2`
- Added the CI e2e job in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- Added marker `data-testid`/state attributes for stable waits
- Added `F10` abort binding to the existing structured `{ kind: "abort" }` action because the UI had no abort path

Verification:
- `pnpm run check` → exit 0, `79 passed`, `532 passed | 2 skipped`
- `pnpm run e2e` → exit 1 before test body:
  `browserType.launch ... MachPortRendezvousServer ... Permission denied (1100)`
- Retried with full Chromium and `--single-process`; same sandbox browser-launch failure.
- 5× green e2e runs: not achieved.

CI diff added:

```diff
+  e2e:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    needs: check
+    env:
+      PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH: ${{ github.workspace }}/.cache/ms-playwright
+      XDG_CACHE_HOME: ${{ github.workspace }}/.cache
+    steps:
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4.2.2
+      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4.1.0
+        with: { version: 10.28.2 }
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4.4.0
+        with: { node-version: "24", cache: pnpm }
+      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
+      - run: pnpm exec playwright install --with-deps chromium
+      - run: pnpm run e2e
```

Environment discoveries:
- `pnpm dlx` also needs `XDG_CACHE_HOME` inside the workspace here.
- Playwright browser install needs `PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH` inside the workspace.
- Chromium launch is blocked by the Codex macOS sandbox Mach-port permissions.

Actual time: ~65m vs 25m, mostly browser install/cache and sandbox launch diagnosis. No commit made.","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

This method returns a `Browser` object, from which you can access the default browser context and its pages. Note that this connection is lower fidelity than the Playwright protocol.\n\n```js\nconst browser = await playwright.chromium.connectOverCDP('http://localhost:9222');\nconst defaultContext = browser.contexts()[0];\nconst page = defaultContext.pages()[0];\n```\n\n```java\nBrowser browser = playwright.chromium().connectOverCDP(\"http://localhost:9222\");\nBrowserContext defaultContext = browser.contexts().get(0);\nPage page = defaultContext.pages().get(0);\n```\n\n```python async\nbrowser = await playwright.chromium.connect_over_cdp(\"http://localhost:9222\")\ndefault_context = browser.contexts[0]\npage = default_context.pages[0]\n```\n\n```python sync\nbrowser = playwright.chromium.connect_over_cdp(\"http://localhost:9222\")\ndefault_context = browser.contexts[0]\npage = default_context.pages[0]\n```\n\n```csharp\nvar browser = await playwright.Chromium.ConnectOverCDPAsync(\"http://localhost:9222\");\nvar defaultContext = browser.Contexts[0];\nvar page = defaultContext.Pages[0];\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Connect to Remote Browser with Playwright Test\n\nSource: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-api/class-testoptions.md\n\nUse `connectOptions` in `playwright.config.ts` to connect to an existing remote browser instance via its WebSocket endpoint instead of launching a new browser.\n\n```js\nimport { defineConfig } from '@playwright/test';\n\nexport default defineConfig({\n  use: {\n    connectOptions: {\n      wsEndpoint: 'ws://localhost:5678',\n    },\n  },\n});\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### BrowserType.connectOverCDP\n\nSource: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/api/class-browsertype.md\n\nThis method attaches Playwright to an existing browser instance using the Chrome DevTools Protocol. The default browser context is accessible via [`method: Browser.contexts`].\n\n```APIDOC\n## BrowserType.connectOverCDP\n\n### Description\nThis method attaches Playwright to an existing browser instance using the Chrome DevTools Protocol. The default browser context is accessible via [`method: Browser.contexts`].\n\n### Signature\nasync method: BrowserType.connectOverCDP(endpointURL, options)\n\n### Parameters\n- **endpointURL** (string) - Required. A CDP websocket endpoint or http url to connect to. For example `http://localhost:9222/` or `ws://127.0.0.1:9222/devtools/browser/387adf4c-243f-4051-a181-46798f4a46f4`.\n- **headers** (Object<string, string>) - Optional. Additional HTTP headers to be sent with connect request.\n- **isLocal** (boolean) - Optional. Tells Playwright that it runs on the same host as the CDP server. It will enable certain optimizations that rely upon the file system being the same between Playwright and the Browser.\n- **slowMo** (float) - Optional. Slows down Playwright operations by the specified amount of milliseconds. Useful so that you can see what is going on. Defaults to 0.\n- **timeout** (float) - Optional. Maximum time in milliseconds to wait for the connection to be established. Defaults to `30000` (30 seconds). Pass `0` to disable timeout.\n- **noDefaults** (boolean) - Optional. When true, Playwright will not apply its default overrides to the existing default browser context. Specifically, [`option: Browser.newContext.acceptDownloads`] is left at the browser's setting, focus emulation is not enabled, and media emulation options (such as [`option: Browser.newContext.colorScheme`], [`option: Browser.newContext.reducedMotion`], [`option: Browser.newContext.forcedColors`], and [`option: Browser.newContext.contrast`]) are not applied. Useful when attaching to a user's daily-driver browser where these overrides would interfere with existing browser state. New contexts created via [`method: Browser.newContext`] are not affected. Defaults to `false`.\n- **artifactsDir** (path) - Optional. If specified, browser artifacts (such as traces and downloads) are saved into this directory.\n\n### Returns\n- **Browser** - An instance of a Browser.\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### PW_TEST_CONNECT_WS_ENDPOINT Environment Variable\n\nSource: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/playwright/packages/playwright/src/index.ts\n\nThe connectOptionsFromEnv function reads PW_TEST_CONNECT_WS_ENDPOINT to enable connection to a persistent remote browser server. This allows test runs to connect to an existing browser instead of launching a new one.\n\n```typescript\nfunction connectOptionsFromEnv() {\n  const wsEndpoint = process.env.PW_TEST_CONNECT_WS_ENDPOINT;\n  if (!wsEndpoint)\n    return undefined;\n  const headers = process.env.PW_TEST_CONNECT_HEADERS ? JSON.parse(process.env.PW_TEST_CONNECT_HEADERS) : undefined;\n  return {\n    wsEndpoint,\n    headers,\n    exposeNetwork: process.env.PW_TEST_CONNECT_EXPOSE_NETWORK,\n  };\n}\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Connect Playwright to remote browsers in Python\n\nSource: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/blob/main/docs/src/test-runners-python.md\n\nConfigure Playwright to connect to a remote browser instance by specifying its WebSocket endpoint in a Pytest fixture.\n\n```python\nimport pytest\n\n@pytest.fixture(scope=\"session\")\ndef connect_options():\n    return {\n        \"wsEndpoint\": \"ws://localhost:8080/ws\"\n    }\n```"}],"structured_content":null},"error":null,"status":"completed"}}

The cross rate stayed below half of same-persona (27.38%). M2 test: `tests/integration/m2.test.ts > keeps tuned mock responsiveness scores at or above the Phase 47 baseline` asserts current mock responsiveness against `tests/eval-baselines/mock-baseline.json`, not inline numbers. |
| difficulty band holds | Phase 47 iteration 1 live tuning improved solvability to 93.33% while preserving the recorded Gate/eval path. M2 test `keeps tuned mock responsiveness scores at or above the Phase 47 baseline` runs the current prompt/mock manifests through the eval suite; `records transition budget instrumentation after a fixture descend` confirms the store records `stairsToPlayableMs`, controller state, served source, and depth pair after a real descend. |
| dungeon diary correctly narrates what happened | M2 test: `keeps the dungeon diary faithful to fixture run sources` checks fixture-run diary entries for narration, close call, kill, death learning, and fallback floor text, and verifies every diary claim is backed by a trace or artifact source. |
| run-to-run memory works | M2 test: `threads run 1 death into run 2 prompt through persisted memory` writes a run-1 death to a temp SQLite DB, reopens persistence, builds the prompt through the real memory selector, and asserts run 2 contains the death memory under `CROSS-RUN MEMORY`. |
| artifacts are inspectable in browser | 54B bridge gap closed with `GET /api/artifacts` list/load over the shared read-only artifact reader. Component/bridge test: `app/components/artifacts/ArtifactViewer.test.ts > loads the Tab artifact pane model through the read-only API bridge` uses a fixture run dir, lists runs, loads the model, rejects an unsafe run id, and renders the Tab artifact pane. |
| host e2e smoke | Per gate scope, browser e2e is orchestrator-owned and not rerun in this sandbox. The M2 report notes the host/orchestrator e2e 5/5 requirement as external evidence to attach during final integration. |

## Deferred Human Session

Standing authorization defers the human session to the morning checklist.

Two-run live session script:

1. Run `pnpm run dev`.
2. Open `http://localhost:3001`.
3. Start run 1 and play it to death.
4. Start run 2 from the same browser profile.
5. On run 2, look for an opening recognition line that references run 1's death.
6. Press `Tab`, switch to `Artifacts`, and verify the artifact tree loads for the current run.

Acceptance note to add after the human pass:

- Recognition line observed: pending.
- Tab -> Artifacts loaded: pending.
- Human feel verdict: pending.

- No commit was made.
- Artifact bridge is read-only: the route exposes list/load only; generation writes remain in the existing Director transport.

## Actual vs Estimate

- Estimate: 45m.
- Actual worker time: ~11m.

item.text: The test diffs are now tight. The report markdown still has formatter-only table churn, so I’m restoring those tables and keeping just the new evidence lines.
M1 is back to a one-line evidence diff. I’m restoring the M3 draft tables to their existing style while retaining the new CI/e2e evidence rows.
Report diffs are narrowed. I’m doing the final diff check before starting the verification suite.

item.aggregated_output:
# E2E Host Runs - 2026-06-12

This records the host-side Playwright evidence for PHASE-55. The Codex macOS
sandbox cannot launch Chromium for this project: the Phase 55 worker observed
`browserType.launch ... MachPortRendezvousServer ... Permission denied (1100)`.
`ENVIRONMENT.md` also records the browser constraint: no usable Chromium in the
sandbox, so browser verification routes through the orchestrator/host.

The GitHub Actions e2e job remains wired separately from this host note. It runs
on `ubuntu-latest` and installs its own Chromium with:

```sh
pnpm exec playwright install --with-deps chromium
```

## Command

```sh
pnpm run e2e
```

## Journey

The single Playwright spec, `e2e/happy-path.spec.ts`, runs one deterministic
mocked-Director path: title screen -> new run -> play floor 1 -> pick up an item
-> inspect inventory -> descend through the transition overlay -> verify depth 2
is playable -> open quest panel -> open/close the Tab diary layer -> abandon the
run -> verify the final diary on the summary screen -> verify the run-index entry.

## Runs

| run | date       | command        | exit code | result |
| --: | ---------- | -------------- | --------: | ------ |
|   1 | 2026-06-12 | `pnpm run e2e` |         0 | pass   |
|   2 | 2026-06-12 | `pnpm run e2e` |         0 | pass   |
|   3 | 2026-06-12 | `pnpm run e2e` |         0 | pass   |
|   4 | 2026-06-12 | `pnpm run e2e` |         0 | pass   |
|   5 | 2026-06-12 | `pnpm run e2e` |         0 | pass   |

Verdict: 5/5 host-side runs passed. The local Codex sandbox run remains skipped
for browser launch permissions; CI coverage is provided by the Ubuntu e2e job.

item.text:
Implemented the CI-truth bundle. No commit made.

Changed:
- Added explicit CI steps for root Vitest, integration, golden, every app Vitest config, and determinism audit in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.
- Added explicit slow-test timeouts only: floorgen `120s`, bots batch `600s`, persona bank determinism tests `300s`.
- Fixed the artifact empty state to `No generation artifacts recorded for this run.`
- Added `docs/evidence/e2e-host-runs.md` with the 2026-06-12 host-side 5/5 e2e record.
- Updated M1/M3 reports with CI wiring, local suite sweep, and e2e evidence.

Verification run:
- `pnpm run check` -> exit 0; typecheck pass, lint pass, Vitest `79 files / 533 passed / 2 skipped`.
- `tests/integration/vitest.config.ts` -> exit 0; `3 files / 9 passed / 2 skipped`.
- `tests/golden/vitest.config.ts` -> exit 0; `1 file / 9 passed`.
- `tests/determinism-audit/vitest.config.ts` -> exit 0; `1 file / 3 passed`.

App configs:
- `app/input` -> `2 files / 9 passed`
- `app/components/artifacts` -> `1 file / 4 passed`
- `app/components/diary` -> `1 file / 2 passed`
- `app/components/grid` -> `1 file / 4 passed`
- `app/components/hud` -> `1 file / 2 passed`
- `app/components/log` -> `1 file / 3 passed`
- `app/components/panels` -> `1 file / 5 passed`
- `app/components/runindex` -> `1 file / 2 passed`
- `app/components/settings` -> `1 file / 4 passed`
- `app/components/title` -> `1 file / 3 passed`
- `app/components/transition` -> `1 file / 5 passed`

E2E local run skipped: Chromium launch is blocked in the Codex macOS sandbox by the documented Mach-port failure; the CI e2e job still installs Chromium on Ubuntu with `pnpm exec playwright install --with-deps chromium`.

Scope deviations: none from the fix brief. I did not edit `PROGRESS.md` because the brief’s owned-file list excluded it. Residual risk: live GitHub Actions green still needs the orchestrator push/confirmation. Environment discovery: the requested Mach-port fact is in the Phase 55 session note, while `ENVIRONMENT.md` only records the broader browser-host constraint.

Generated evidence left intact: `runs/sessions/ci-truth.jsonl` updated during the session, and `pnpm run check` generated new `runs/bot-*` artifacts.

Actual time: ~70m vs 45m estimate; overrun was mostly full-estate local verification and cleaning up formatter noise back to narrow diffs.
